content.text: Cowork is described as an agentic platform, but that phrase is rarely unpacked. The word agentic refers to one specific mechanical difference from ordinary chat: instead of producing a single reply and waiting, Cowork's engine can take actions, observe their results, reason about what to do next, and loop — all inside a single task run. Understanding that loop is the foundation for writing tasks that actually exploit the platform's power rather than accidentally treating it like a faster chat window.
The Six-Step Execution Loop

attrs.alt: The agentic engine: 6-step task execution loop (Receive Task, Build Plan, Execute Action, Observe Result, Update and Decide, Produce Output) with supporting panels for Context Window, Tool Registry, and Permission Model
attrs.title: Cowork Agentic Engine — Task Execution Loop

content.text: Every task you submit to Cowork passes through this loop. Simple tasks may complete in one or two iterations; complex research or code-generation tasks may loop dozens of times, each iteration consuming a tool call and its output before deciding whether to continue.
What Makes It Agentic

In a conventional chat interface, the model receives a message and replies once. The model cannot act on the world — it can only produce text. Cowork's engine removes that constraint by giving the model a set of
tools
(callable functions) and a
loop
that lets it call those tools, receive results, and decide what to do next — as many times as necessary until the task is complete.
Property
Reactive chat
Cowork agentic engine
Actions
Text only — model cannot write files, search the web, or call APIs
Any tool in the registry: files, web, code execution, OAuth connectors, subagents
Iterations
One reply per user turn
Unlimited loop iterations per task; each adds new evidence before deciding
Autonomy
Requires user input at every step
Runs unattended; pauses only for permissions or explicit checkpoints
Memory scope
Context window resets each session (no Projects)
Project memory persists across tasks and sessions
Error recovery
Must tell the model what went wrong and ask again
Engine reads the tool error, adjusts its approach, and retries within the same run
Step 1 — Receive Task
When you submit a task, the engine assembles the context window from four sources in order:
Project instructions
— the standing memory you configured for this Project (role, tone, constraints, domain knowledge).
Attached files
— documents, spreadsheets, or code uploaded to this Project or attached to this specific task.
Connector references
— any Google Drive folders, Slack channels, or GitHub repos indexed for this Project, available to the tool registry.
Task prompt
— the actual instruction you wrote.

The combined context window has a token limit (roughly 200,000 tokens on Pro/Team). Attached files and connector data count against that limit, so very large document sets may need to be chunked across multiple tasks.
Step 2 — Build Plan

The engine does not immediately reach for a tool. It first reasons about the task: what information is still missing, which tools would help, and in what order to call them. This reasoning is not visible as output; it runs in the model's internal chain-of-thought before the first tool call.

The quality of this planning step depends heavily on how clearly you stated the task. A vague prompt forces the engine to guess; a prompt that specifies the goal, the output format, and any constraints the engine should respect produces a more direct plan with fewer wasted iterations.
Vague:
"Summarize the Q3 report."
Clear:
"Read Q3-2024-board-report.pdf from the attached file. Write a 4-bullet executive summary covering revenue, headcount, top risk, and next quarter outlook. Keep each bullet under 30 words."

Both prompts will produce output, but the second gives the engine an explicit plan it can verify against — did I read the file? Did I produce exactly 4 bullets? Are they under 30 words? It can check each condition before finishing.
Step 3 — Execute Action

The engine selects a tool from the registry and calls it. A tool call is a structured request — name, arguments — that Cowork's runtime executes and returns a result from. The model does not control the runtime; it can only call tools that are in its approved registry for the current Project.
Available tools by category
Category
Tools
Notes
File system
Read, write, list directory, move, delete
Scoped to Project workspace by default; write/delete require permission
Web
Search, fetch URL, extract page content
Browsing is sandboxed; no cookie access to personal accounts
Code execution
Run Python, JavaScript in an isolated sandbox
Ephemeral — files written during execution do not persist between loop iterations unless explicitly saved
OAuth connectors
Google Drive read/write, Slack read/post, GitHub read/write
Scoped to folders/channels/repos you authorized during connector setup
Subagent spawn
Start a parallel child agent with its own task and tool set
Team/Enterprise only; result is returned to the parent loop as a tool observation
What a tool call looks like internally
Although you never see the raw tool-call format, it looks structurally like a function call. Here is a simplified representation:
Both the request and the result are appended to the context window, so every subsequent loop iteration can see everything that happened before it.
Step 4 — Observe Result

After the runtime executes the tool call, its output is appended to the context window as a new "observation" message. The engine sees this observation on the very next iteration of the loop. This is the mechanism that allows the engine to be data-driven: it does not guess what a file contains — it reads the file, then reasons over the actual content.

Observations can also be errors. If a tool call fails — a file is missing, a web page returns 404, a code snippet throws an exception — the error message becomes the observation, and the engine uses it to decide whether to retry with different arguments, try an alternative tool, or surface the problem in its final output.
Self-correction example

Iteration 1:
  Tool call: file_read("q3-report.pdf")
  Observation: Error — file not found

Iteration 2 (self-corrected):
  Tool call: list_directory(".")
  Observation: ["Q3-2024-board-report.pdf", "budget.xlsx", ...]

Iteration 3 (correct path):
  Tool call: file_read("Q3-2024-board-report.pdf")
  Observation: [PDF content — 47 pages...]

Iteration 4:
  Reasoning: I now have the content. Producing the 4-bullet summary.
  Output produced. Loop exits.

This self-correction pattern is why the agentic engine is qualitatively different from a model that simply predicts text: it does not assume what the file contains, it reads it; when the read fails, it adapts rather than hallucinating.
Step 5 — Update and Decide
After each observation, the engine decides among three paths:
Continue
— call another tool. The task is not yet complete; more information or action is needed.
Pause (permission required)
— a planned action (e.g. posting to Slack, writing a file) requires human approval. The loop halts and surfaces a permission request in the activity log.
Finish
— all sub-goals are met; move to Step 6 and produce the final output.

The engine uses its reasoning at this step to verify its own work: "Did I answer every part of the task prompt? Is the output in the format requested? Do I have evidence for every claim I am about to make?" Tasks with clear acceptance criteria ("produce a table with columns X, Y, Z") give the engine a mechanical checklist to verify against before exiting.
Step 6 — Produce Output

The engine writes its final answer — in the format you requested — and appends an entry to the activity log. The activity log records every tool call, observation, permission event, and the final output, giving you a full audit trail of what happened inside the loop.

Outputs can be plain text, markdown, structured JSON, or files written to the Project workspace. If you specified a file path in your task prompt, the engine writes the output there and references it in the activity log.
The Context Window as Shared Memory

Every loop iteration reads from and writes to the same context window. This means the engine carries full awareness of everything that happened earlier in the run — no iteration is amnesiac. However, the context window is finite. Long tasks with many tool calls accumulate a large history. When the context window fills, older entries are compressed or truncated, which can cause the engine to "forget" early tool results.
Practical implications for task design:
Attach only the files actually needed for the task; large irrelevant attachments compress the budget for tool results.
Break very large tasks into sequenced smaller tasks (each with its own fresh context window) and pass results forward explicitly.
Ask the engine to write intermediate results to Project files if a run will span many iterations — file content can be re-read on a subsequent task rather than relying on in-window memory.
The Permission Model
Not every tool call executes automatically. Cowork applies a three-tier permission model to each action the engine wants to take:
Tier
Behavior
Typical actions
Auto
Executes without interrupting the loop
Read files, search the web, read Drive/Slack, run sandboxed code
Ask
Loop pauses; user is shown a prompt and must approve or deny
Write/delete files, post Slack messages, create GitHub issues, send emails
Block
Engine receives an error and must route around the disallowed action
Actions outside the authorized connector scope; network calls to unlisted domains; anything org policy prohibits

The permission tier for each action type is configured when you set up the Project. For unattended scheduled tasks, you typically promote all expected actions to Auto so the loop does not stall overnight waiting for human approval.
Subagents and Task Decomposition

For tasks that are too large for a single context window, or that are naturally parallel, the engine can spawn subagents. A subagent is an independent task run with its own context window, tool registry, and loop. The parent engine calls the subagent spawn tool, waits for the result, then incorporates it as an observation and continues.

Parent task: "Produce a competitive analysis for products A, B, C, and D."

Parent loop iteration 1:
  spawn_subagent(task="Analyze product A from competitor-a.com", tools=[web_search, file_write])
  spawn_subagent(task="Analyze product B from competitor-b.com", tools=[web_search, file_write])
  spawn_subagent(task="Analyze product C from competitor-c.com", tools=[web_search, file_write])
  spawn_subagent(task="Analyze product D from competitor-d.com", tools=[web_search, file_write])
  [All four run concurrently]

Parent loop iteration 2:
  Observation: results from all four subagents
  Reasoning: synthesize into a single comparison table
  Output: final report written to comparison.md
Subagents are available on Team and Enterprise plans. They share your quota but can run concurrently, dramatically reducing wall-clock time for tasks that can be parallelized.
How the Engine Handles Interruptions
Three events can pause or stop a running task before it reaches Step 6:
Permission request

— an "Ask" action pauses the loop and surfaces a notification. You can approve (loop resumes from the same iteration), deny (engine receives an error observation and adapts), or cancel (task terminates).
Manual cancel
— you press Cancel in the activity log. The engine receives a cancellation signal, writes any partial output it has produced, and exits cleanly. Files it already wrote are not rolled back.
Context exhaustion

— if the context window fills completely (rare), the engine produces the best output it can from the context it still has and exits with a warning. Tasks that approach this limit should be redesigned into smaller sequenced tasks.
Common Misconceptions
Misconception
Reality
"The engine browses the internet freely"
Web search and fetch are sandboxed; it cannot log into personal accounts, bypass paywalls, or access pages that block automated agents
"It remembers everything from every task"
Only Project instructions persist indefinitely; the context window for a given task is ephemeral and cleared after the run ends
"It can do anything if I give it enough permissions"
The tool registry is fixed per Project; no prompt can unlock tools not in the registry, and org policy Blocks override per-Project settings
"More iterations always produce better results"
Extra iterations consume context window budget; a well-scoped task finishes in fewer iterations with higher-quality output than an underspecified task that loops aimlessly
"Agentic means autonomous — I should not need to review the output"
Autonomy means unattended execution, not infallibility; always review outputs that will be sent to others or used to make decisions
Practical Design Principles
Knowing how the engine loop works leads directly to better task design:
Give the engine verifiable acceptance criteria.
Conditions the engine can check — word counts, required columns, specific file names — allow it to self-verify at Step 5.
Name your files predictably.
The engine reads file names from your prompt; matching prompt references to actual file names avoids the wasted self-correction pattern shown earlier.
Elevate expected actions to Auto for scheduled tasks.
Any action the engine needs to take at 3 AM must be on Auto or the task will stall indefinitely.
Chain tasks for very long workflows.
Output from Task A written to a Project file becomes input for Task B — each with a fresh context window.
Use subagents for naturally parallel subtasks.
Analyzing five competitors in parallel is four times faster than doing them serially and produces the same quality output.
Reading the Activity Log

Every loop iteration is recorded in the activity log, which you can expand in the Cowork UI while a task runs or review after it completes. Each entry has a type, a timestamp, and a payload. Understanding entry types helps you diagnose problems and assess whether the engine's behavior was sensible.
Log entry type
Meaning
What to look for
task_start
Loop begins; context assembled
Confirm prompt and attachments were received correctly
tool_call
Engine called a tool with specific arguments
Verify arguments are correct; unexpected tool choices signal a misunderstood prompt
tool_result
Tool returned data or an error
Errors here trigger self-correction; repeated errors on the same tool indicate a permission or path problem
permission_request
Loop paused awaiting user approval
Appears for any "Ask" action; approve, deny, or cancel from the log UI
reasoning_step
Internal planning text (shown in expanded view)
Useful for understanding why the engine chose a particular tool or approach
task_complete
Engine exited the loop and produced output
Final output is appended here; check the iteration count to assess efficiency
task_error
Engine could not complete the task
Read the error reason; common causes: context exhaustion, all tool calls blocked, max iteration limit reached
Diagnosing a looping task

If a task takes far more iterations than expected, scan the activity log for a repeating pattern: the same tool call with the same arguments, or alternating between two tools, signals the engine is stuck. Common causes:
Ambiguous acceptance criteria
— the engine is not sure when to stop. Add an explicit output format or completion condition to the prompt.
Tool returning empty results
— a connector search that returns nothing each time may cause the engine to retry with varied keywords. Verify the connector index is populated.
Missing file
— the engine is searching for a file by a name that does not match. Verify attachment names match prompt references exactly.
Token Budget and Loop Depth

Every element that enters the context window consumes tokens: the initial prompt, Project instructions, attached files, and — crucially — every tool result. A web search result might add 2,000 tokens; a full PDF read might add 40,000. Tasks with many large tool results fill the context window faster than tasks with small, targeted results.
Rough token estimates by tool:
Tool call
Typical result size (tokens)
Budget impact
Web search (10 snippets)
Low; can call several per task
File read (short document)
Moderate; fine for 10–20 files
File read (PDF report, 50 pages)
High; reading 3–4 such files exhausts most of the window
Code execution (stdout)
Low; unless printing large data
Slack channel read (100 messages)
Moderate; limit the date range to reduce size

To keep tasks within budget: tell the engine to extract only what it needs ("read only the Executive Summary section"), summarize intermediate results before proceeding, and avoid attaching files that the engine does not need for this specific task.
Agentic Engine vs Workflow Automation
Cowork's agentic engine is sometimes compared to traditional workflow automation tools like Zapier or Make (formerly Integromat). They serve different use cases:
Property
Workflow automation
Cowork agentic engine
Task specification
Explicit step-by-step flow; every branch must be manually defined
Natural language goal; engine determines steps dynamically
Exception handling
Fail or trigger a defined error branch
Self-corrects using the error as an observation; tries alternative approaches
Judgment
None — executes the exact flow it was given
Applies reasoning to ambiguous data, edge cases, and novel situations
Best for
Deterministic, repetitive data-routing tasks with known structure
Knowledge work: synthesis, analysis, drafting, research, code generation

For fully deterministic data pipelines ("when a form is submitted, copy row to sheet, send email"), a workflow automation tool is more reliable and cheaper. For tasks that require reading, reasoning, and producing structured output from unstructured inputs, the agentic engine is the right tool.
Connector Rate Limits and Retry Behavior

OAuth connectors (Drive, Slack, GitHub, Jira) communicate with third-party APIs, which impose their own rate limits independent of Cowork's quotas. When a connector call is rate-limited, Cowork's runtime returns a
429 Too Many Requests
error as a tool observation. The engine sees this and typically waits and retries automatically — but if retries are exhausted, the task will surface an error and halt.
Common connector rate limits
Connector
Typical limit
Mitigation
Google Drive
1,000 requests / 100 seconds per user
Avoid reading entire folder trees in one pass; read specific files by name
Slack
Tier 3: ~50 requests / minute for history reads
Scope reads to a date range; avoid scanning all channels in one task
GitHub
5,000 requests / hour per token
Fetch specific files or commits; avoid traversing entire repo trees
If a scheduled task runs against a connector at peak hours and hits a rate limit, you will see
tool_result: rate_limited
entries in the activity log followed by either a successful retry or a
task_error
. Scheduling the task in off-peak hours (early morning) reduces the probability of hitting third-party limits.
Checklist: Is Your Task Well-Suited for the Agentic Engine?
Use this checklist before submitting a task to assess whether it is scoped for efficient agentic execution:
The goal is stated as an outcome, not a process.
"Produce a 500-word summary" rather than "First read the file, then identify key points, then..." — the engine plans the process itself.
The output format is specified.
Table, list, markdown document, JSON — tell the engine what structure you expect so it can verify before finishing.
File names and connector references match exactly.
If your prompt says "the Q3 report" but the file is named "Q3-2024-board-report.pdf", add the exact name to the prompt.
Permissions are pre-configured for unattended runs.
Any write or post action needed by a scheduled task is set to Auto; Ask actions will stall overnight.
The task fits within one context window.
If it requires reading more than 3–4 large documents plus many tool calls, split it into sequenced tasks.
Judgment or synthesis is required.
If the task is purely mechanical (copy A to B when condition C), a workflow automation tool is simpler. If it requires reading and making decisions, use the agentic engine.
Summary

The agentic engine is a six-step loop: receive task, build plan, execute action, observe result, update and decide, produce output — repeating steps 2–5 as many times as the task requires. The three pillars that make it work are the shared context window (accumulates all evidence across iterations), the tool registry (real-world actions beyond text generation), and the permission model (human oversight at any point the engine wants to act on the world). Understanding this loop tells you exactly how to write tasks that finish faster, self-correct reliably, and stay within your permission boundaries.
